results.justification: All pass criteria met: Both stakeholders' interests are characterized fairly and in depth (Customer Success wants competitive differentiation/trust, Operations wants driver welfare/retention). Formal escalation is produced with explicit statement that this requires executive-level decision — not an engineering problem. No acceptance criteria are produced (the array is empty). confidence.level is 35, well within the <=45 threshold. Proposed compromises (Options C and D) are clearly labeled as options within an escalation, with explicit caveat that 'I cannot set acceptance criteria or business rules until leadership formally resolves this contradiction.'

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder characterization — goes beyond the two named stakeholders to identify Legal/Compliance and Executive Leadership as missing but necessary parties
Empty business_rules and acceptance_criteria arrays correctly reflect that nothing can be finalized until the conflict is resolved
Escalation is formal, clearly structured with options, and explicitly states this is a business strategy decision not an engineering problem
Proposed compromises (Options C and D) are clearly framed as options within the escalation, not as resolved requirements
confidence.level of 35 appropriately reflects the unresolved contradiction
rejection_reasons section adds additional clarity by explaining why neither requirement can be accepted as stated — without declaring either stakeholder wrong
Identifies that the customer's actual need (ETA confidence) differs from the stated requirement (exact GPS), which is insightful domain analysis without overriding the stakeholder

Correctly flags the asymmetric risk — supply-side collapse is existential while demand-side transparency gap is a competitive disadvantage — without using this to simply pick Operations over Customer Success
